content.assessment: Networked retention directly targets the network-resilience criterion by pooling capacity across villages rather than relying on any single site's enrollment viability, and combining kindergarten and primary provision addresses parallel capacity gaps jointly. This is the proposal most explicitly structured around inter-municipal cooperation as a network design principle. Yet its central vulnerability, documented directly in this lens's evidence, is that cooperative network design is threatened by the same informal inter-municipal competition for pupils that undermines trust between neighboring settlements, and that no statutory procedure currently governs such networked arrangements, leaving implementation legally ambiguous.
opportunities: Historical Hungarian closure-cascade evidence (Nógrád county) shows enrollment thresholds around 35-65 pupils trigger association pressure; a networked model could formalize resource-sharing before such thresholds force reactive mergers (F-live-school_network_planning-05)
strengths: Pools capacity and pedagogical resources across multiple villages, directly addressing diseconomies of scale in isolated small schools

Combining kindergarten and primary provision in a single hub addresses two parallel capacity/access gaps with one structural mechanism (relates to broader coverage-gap patterns, cf. F-live-school_network_planning-05 on enrollment thresholds triggering association pressure)
threats: Continuing rural population decline and reduced rural in-migration shrink the shared pupil pool that networked clusters depend on for collective viability (F-live-school_network_planning-01, F-live-school_network_planning-02)

Contested comparative evidence on closure-depopulation links means the assumed benefit of avoiding closure (versus the effects of network restructuring itself) is not settled (F-live-school_network_planning-06, F-live-school_network_planning-10)
weaknesses: Documented Hungarian evidence shows inter-municipal competition (informal pupil-pull dynamics) actively undermines cooperative network arrangements, directly threatening the cluster model's premise (F-live-school_network_planning-09)

No dedicated statutory procedure exists for network/closure decisions, creating implementation ambiguity that a purely voluntary cooperative model cannot resolve on its own (F-live-school_network_planning-04)
